name: Iridium Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Iridium. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Iridium surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Fundamental Nd:YAG wavelength with good Iridium absorption

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for effective Iridium surface cleaning

pulseWidth.description: Short pulse duration for efficient ablation with minimal heat diffusion

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for efficient area coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contamination removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning results

power.description: Typical range for industrial Nd:YAG laser cleaning of refractory metals like Iridium, starting low due to high reflectivity (~90% at 1064 nm) to avoid excessive heating; max limited to prevent melting (mp 2446°C). Recommended start at 100 W for contamination removal. Source: IPG Photonics specs for YLR series; conservative estimate from metal cleaning studies (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds balance fluence and coverage; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., oxides), faster for light surface prep. Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal Iridium cleaning without thermal buildup, considering low thermal conductivity (14.8 W/m·K). Source: CleanLaser equipment manuals; industry standards for 1064 nm cleaning (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective industrial cleaning; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for minimal HAZ on heat-sensitive Iridium, but ns sufficient due to high melting point. Recommended 20 ns to limit heat diffusion. Source: Laser physics reviews (e.g., Prog. Quantum Electron. 2019); ns common in metal ablation studies.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed cleaning to achieve high average power without excessive heat accumulation; higher rates speed up process but risk thermal effects on reflective Iridium. Recommended 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Source: Academic literature on Nd:YAG cleaning (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2017); trade-off noted in high-rep-rate studies.

energy_density.description: Fluence below Iridium damage threshold (~3-6 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to reflectivity); typical for contaminant ablation without substrate damage. Recommended 2 J/cm² for safe removal of light contaminants. Source: Ablation threshold studies for PGMs (J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 2021); conservative for Iridium's properties.

spot_size.description: Smaller spots for fine detail on valuable Iridium surfaces, larger for broad areas; 1064 nm beam focused tightly for precision. Recommended 200 μm for uniform cleaning without over-ablation. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf lasers; application-based ranges in industrial cleaning (Lasers Manuf. Mater. Process. 2019).

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy layers; Iridium's durability allows 2 passes without damage. Recommended 2 for complete removal in surface prep. Source: Industry case studies on metal cleaning (e.g., SPIE Proc. 2022); practice for refractory metals.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or excessive overlap heating; 50% optimal for Iridium to minimize heat-affected zones while covering reflective surfaces evenly. Source: Process optimization studies (Opt. Express 2018); typical range 30-80% in scanning laser cleaning standards.
